summary: Mexican surrealist painter known for her many self-portraits, often featuring her prominent unibrow and monkeys. Married to muralist Diego Rivera. Her art was deeply influenced by a devastating bus accident at age 18, chronic pain, and her turbulent marriage. Andre Breton described her work as 'a ribbon around a bomb.' She lived and worked at the Blue House (Casa Azul) in Coyoacan.

works.name: The Broken Column (1944)
works.description: One of Kahlo's best-known self-portraits, depicting her with a cracked Ionic column replacing her spine, her body held together by a medical brace, and nails piercing her skin. Represents the lifelong pain she suffered from a bus accident at age 18. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Shows Kahlo with a broken/shattered column in place of her spine, held by a medical brace
examples: this self-portrait, which shows the artist studded with crude nails and the titular shattered object held in place of a spine by a medical brace
Another self-portrait showed this artist's spine as the title Broken Column
nails piercing the skin of its subject, who is constrained by a body brace and has a broken column instead of a spine

clues.clue: Represents/symbolizes the bus accident she experienced at age 18

works.name: The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (1939)
works.description: Commissioned by Clare Boothe Luce as a memorial portrait for socialite Dorothy Hale, who jumped from the Hampshire House. The painting shows Hale falling through clouds and lying on the sidewalk. Isamu Noguchi removed the dedication. Kahlo included bloodstains on the frame and text at the bottom in the votive painting style. Wikipedia

works.name: Henry Ford Hospital (1932)
works.description: A votive-style painting on sheet metal depicting Kahlo on a hospital bed after a miscarriage, with six objects (including a snail, orchid, pelvic bone, and plaster model) connected by red cords/ribbons to her figure. Relates to her inability to bear children after her bus accident. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Kahlo is identified in clues and common biographical information. She is consistently identified as the unibrowed, Mexican wife of Diego Rivera who painted many self-portraits featuring monkeys. Her art was deeply influenced by a bus accident at age 18 that caused lifelong pain and prevented her from having children. She lived at the Blue House (Casa Azul) in Coyoacan. Andre Breton called her work 'a ribbon around a bomb' and arranged her first solo exhibition in New York in 1938. She kept parrots and monkeys as pets. Her diary, kept during the last ten years of her life, includes surrealistic watercolor illustrations and was published with an introduction by Carlos Fuentes.

comprehensive_summary:
Frida Kahlo (born Magdalena Carmen Frida Kahlo y Calderon) is a Mexican painter universally identified in quizbowl by her marriage to muralist Diego Rivera, her prominent unibrow, and her numerous self-portraits featuring monkeys and flower crowns. Her art was profoundly shaped by a devastating bus accident at age 18 that left her in lifelong pain and unable to bear children.

Her most frequently clued painting is The Broken Column (1944), which shows a cracked Ionic column replacing her spine, her body studded with nails and constrained by a medical brace — a direct representation of her suffering from the bus accident. The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (1939), commissioned by Clare Boothe Luce, depicts socialite Dorothy Hale falling from Hampshire House through cottony clouds onto a sidewalk; Isamu Noguchi controversially removed the dedication, and Kahlo included bloodstains on the frame in the tradition of Mexican votive paintings. What the Water Gave Me (1938) is an autobiographical painting showing Kahlo's bare legs in a bathtub with dreamlike images floating on the surface, including a skyscraper rising from a smoldering volcano, a dead bird on a gnarled tree, a naked woman being choked, insects crawling along a tightrope wrapping around the neck of a dead woman, a happy skeleton on a hill, and a married couple believed to be Kahlo's parents. The pose of two lesbian lovers in this painting is echoed in Kahlo's later work Two Nudes in the Forest. The painting also lends its name to a Florence and the Machine song.

Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (1940) is her most recognizable giveaway — showing her against green leaves with a thorn necklace whose hummingbird pendant draws blood from her neck, with a black cat and monkey on her shoulders and dragonflies and butterflies hovering above. The thorn necklace and bird ornament were reportedly inspired by Aztec gods. The Two Fridas (1939) is a double self-portrait showing one Frida in a white Victorian gown and another in a traditional Mexican dress, connected by a blood vessel between their exposed hearts, with blood dripping from scissors onto the white dress. Henry Ford Hospital (1932) depicts Kahlo on a hospital bed after a miscarriage, with six objects (snail, orchid, pelvic bone, plaster model, floating dead baby/fetus) connected by red cords to her figure, painted in the votive retablo style on sheet metal.

The Wounded Deer (1946) is a retablo-inspired self-portrait showing Kahlo's face on the body of a young deer pierced by nine arrows in a forest clearing. The deer was modeled by her pet named Granizo, and the painting contains the word 'carma' under a single large broken branch in the foreground. The Dream (The Bed) (1940), also known as My Bed, depicts a skeleton with flowers in its heart and dynamite in its legs lying on a bunk bed above a sleeping self-portrait of Kahlo. The Love Embrace of the Universe (1949) shows Kahlo cradling a nude, baby-like Diego Rivera, whose forehead displays a third eye.

Viva la Vida, Watermelons (1954) was her final painting, a still life of sliced watermelons inscribed with the phrase 'Viva la Vida.' Diego Rivera also painted watermelons as his final artwork. The Wounded Table (1940, lost since 1955) is Kahlo's largest painting, flanked by red curtains, showing Kahlo seated next to Cristina's two children, a dynamite-studded giant, a papier-mache skeleton, and a Nayarit figurine. Other notable works include A Few Small Nips (bloodstains on the frame; title from murderer's words), Moses (images of mitosis and fertilization), My Nurse and I (Kahlo as infant with masked wet nurse), Tree of Hope Remain Strong (divided into day and night sides), Self-Portrait with Cropped Hair (oversized suit with music lyrics about hair), Self-Portrait as a Tehuana and Diego and I (Rivera's face on Kahlo's forehead), and The Bride Frightened at Seeing Life Opened (doll in a wedding dress, owl, split papaya).

Biographically, Andre Breton described her work as 'a ribbon around a bomb' and arranged her first solo exhibition in New York in 1938, during a four-month tour of Mexico where he visited Leon Trotsky. She was categorized as a Surrealist against her will and profiled in the condescending article 'Wife of the Master Mural Painter Gleefully Dabbles in Works of Art.' She lived at the Blue House (Casa Azul) in Coyoacan, where she kept parrots and monkeys as pets and amassed a collection of votive paintings. Her diary, kept during the last ten years of her life and published with an introduction by Carlos Fuentes, features surrealistic watercolor illustrations and contains the famous quote 'Feet, what do I need you for, if I have wings to fly?' The last image in the diary is of an angel, which biographer Hayden Herrera interprets as the Angel of Death; she also called herself 'the Ancient Concealer' in it. Diego's affair with Kahlo's sister Cristina and Kahlo's subsequent affair with Leon Trotsky at the Blue House are frequently clued biographical details. She also had an affair with sculptor Isamu Noguchi in 1936 when he came to Mexico to paint a mural for Rivera. She played 'exquisite corpse' with Lucienne Bloch. She was photographed by Lola Alvarez Bravo, and her works were photographed by Tina Modotti. She described Diego Rivera as 'a boy bullfrog standing on his hind legs.' She called Mexico City's artistic emigres, including Remedios Varo, 'the bitches of Paris.'

cards.front: Painting: a cracked/broken column replaces the subject's spine, held by a medical brace, with nails piercing the skin
Painting: symbolizes a bus accident at age 18 through nails and a shattered spinal column
Painting: commissioned by Clare Boothe Luce, shows a socialite falling from Hampshire House through clouds onto the sidewalk
cards.back: The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: Isamu Noguchi removed the dedication from this painting commissioned by Clare Boothe Luce
cards.back: The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: the artist included bloodstains on the frame of this painting about a socialite's death, with text at the bottom in votive style
cards.back: The Suicide of Dorothy Hale (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: autobiographical work showing bare legs in a bathtub with macabre dreamlike images floating on the water
cards.back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: a skyscraper rising out of/emerging from a volcano appears in this bathtub painting
cards.back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: a dead canary/bird on top of a gnarled tree appears in this autobiographical bathtub painting
cards.back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: the subject wears a thorn necklace with a dead hummingbird pendant that draws blood from her neck, with a cat and monkey on her shoulders
cards.back: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (Frida Kahlo)
cards.work: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (1940)

cards.front: Painting: dragonflies and butterflies hover above the subject, who is draped in thorns against a backdrop of green leaves
cards.back: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (Frida Kahlo)
cards.work: Self-Portrait with Thorn Necklace and Hummingbird (1940)

cards.front: Painting: double self-portrait — one figure in a white Victorian gown and another in a traditional Mexican dress, connected by a blood vessel between their exposed hearts
Painting: blood drips from scissors onto a white dress; a severed vein connects two versions of the artist
Painting: votive-style work on sheet metal showing six objects (snail, orchid, pelvic bone, plaster model) connected by red cords to the artist on a hospital bed after a miscarriage
Painting: the artist's final work; sliced watermelons inscribed with the phrase 'Viva la Vida'
Painting: monkey named Fulang-Chang appears behind the subject's shoulder against green leaves; given with a mirror to Mary Sklar
Painting: Diego Rivera's face appears on the subject's forehead in self-portraits, including one with a white lace headdress of Tehuantepec
cards.back: Self-Portrait as a Tehuana / Diego and I (Frida Kahlo)
cards.work: Self-Portrait as a Tehuana / Diego and I

cards.front: Painting: subject wears an oversized suit surrounded by cut hair, with a bar of music and lyrics 'Look, if I loved you it was because of your hair'
Painting: lost since 1955; depicts the artist between a tiny-headed dynamite-studded giant, a papier-mache skeleton, and a brown Nayarit figure
Painting: divided into day and night sides; the subject holds a flag reading 'tree of hope, remain strong'
cards.back: Tree of Hope, Remain Strong (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: depicts a mutilated woman; title comes from the words of her murderer; bloodstains painted on the frame
Painting: depicts the Sun, images of mitosis, fertilization, historical figures, and a baby in the womb
Painting: the artist appears as an infant suckling from a bare-breasted woman wearing a ritual mask
Painting: two large hands (one with an eye in its palm) envelop the subject; a dove spreads its wings over the Earth and Moon
cards.back: Marxism Will Give Health to the Sick (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: a dress suspended from a toilet and a trophy over skyscrapers; includes a lion statue and George Washington painting in a Detroit street
Artist: Andre Breton described this artist's work as 'a ribbon around a bomb' and arranged her first solo exhibition in New York in 1938
Artist: lived at the Blue House (Casa Azul) in Coyoacan, where she kept parrots and a collection of votive paintings (retablos)
Artist: her husband's affair with her sister Cristina led her to sleep with Leon Trotsky during his stay at the Blue House
Artist: her diary, kept during the last 10 years of her life, features surrealistic watercolor illustrations and was published with an introduction by Carlos Fuentes
Artist: wrote 'Feet, what do I need you for, if I have wings to fly?' below an illustration of her amputated feet in her diary
Artist: categorized as a Surrealist against her will; profiled in a condescending article titled 'Wife of the Master Mural Painter Gleefully Dabbles in Works of Art'
Artist: called Mexico City's artistic emigres, including Remedios Varo, 'the bitches of Paris'
Artist: called herself 'the Ancient Concealer' in her diary, which was written in a journal dubiously believed to have once belonged to John Keats
Painting: the artist's face on the body of a young deer pierced by nine arrows in a forest clearing; the deer was modeled by her pet named Granizo
Painting: contains the word 'carma' under a single large broken branch in the foreground
Painting: a skeleton with flowers in its heart and dynamite in its legs lies on a bunk bed above a sleeping self-portrait of the artist
Painting: shows the artist holding her husband as a nude, baby-like figure with a third eye open on his forehead
cards.back: The Love Embrace of the Universe (Frida Kahlo)
cards.work: The Love Embrace of the Universe (1949)

cards.front: Painting: various insects crawl along a tightrope wrapping around the neck of a dead woman and the wrist of a masked man
cards.back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: the pose of two lesbian lovers in this bathtub painting is echoed in the later work Two Nudes in the Forest
cards.back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: the title object is also a Florence and the Machine song; two feet, one bloody and deformed, jut from grey water
cards.back: What the Water Gave Me (Frida Kahlo)

cards.front: Painting: Kahlo's largest work; the two children of her sister Cristina stand next to the title object; flanked by red curtains; Kahlo's arm is continuous with that of a Nayarit figurine
Painting: six objects including a floating dead baby, a disembodied pelvic bone, and an orchid are connected by red cords to the artist on a hospital bed
Artist: had an affair with Isamu Noguchi in 1936 while he was in Mexico to create a mural for her husband
Artist: played 'exquisite corpse' with Lucienne Bloch, creating an image of 'Frida' with a phallic fig leaf and 'Diego' with drooping breasts
Artist: described her husband as 'a boy bullfrog standing on his hind legs'
Artist: flower crowns and floral headdresses frequently appear in her self-portraits, along with her iconic unibrow

recursive_suggestions: Diego Rivera — Kahlo's husband, frequently co-clued; his murals (Man at the Crossroads, Detroit Industry, Sunday Afternoon in Alameda) appear often
Remedios Varo — fellow Mexican surrealist, connected to Kahlo as one of the 'bitches of Paris'
Andre Breton — Surrealist critic who arranged Kahlo's first exhibition
Isamu Noguchi — sculptor connected to Kahlo via The Suicide of Dorothy Hale
Mexican votive paintings / retablos — Kahlo's collection and influence on her style
